title: A Review of Martin (2000)
subtitle: From Notation to Writing and the threshold between descriptive systems and written text
summary: Martin is correct and useful as an early SignWriting-positive comparison, but evidence-limited as a full-path claim.
synopsis: Martin's 2000 paper is an important sympathetic comparison rather than an opponent text. It clearly sees the limitations of linear and alphabetic assumptions for signed writing and takes SignWriting seriously as a real public writing possibility. Its layer-level insight remains valuable, while its broader historical, software, and learnability claims remain evidence-limited.

slides.title: Sympathetic Comparison
slides.summary: Martin is an important SignWriting-positive comparison that recognizes signed writing as a real writing problem.
slides.speech: This review treats Martin as an important sympathetic comparison. The paper recognizes that signed writing is a real writing problem, sees how linear assumptions distort analysis, and takes SignWriting seriously as more than notation.
slides.notes: Make clear this is not an opponent text.

image_prompts.concrete: Comparative review slide placing Martin 2000 beside SignWriting with notation, bilingual mediation, and writing-system claims separated
image_prompts.abstract: A sympathetic comparison with clear boundaries between notation and writing

slides.title: Where The Comparison Overextends
slides.summary: The paper sometimes overstates what SignWriting had already proven and relies on historically dated software and learnability claims.
slides.speech: The limits are mostly matters of overextension. Martin sometimes overstates what SignWriting had already proven, and some software, typing, and learnability claims are historically useful but dated.
slides.notes: Keep this fair to a paper from 2000.

image_prompts.concrete: Critical comparison slide showing a notation-to-writing argument extending beyond the evidence available for its system
image_prompts.abstract: A comparison line extending past its evidence boundary

slides.title: Framework Reading
slides.summary: Through the framework, Martin is strong on the writing problem and spatial organization, but less precise about evidence and the written sign as the primary unit.
bullets: Written sign not always centered cleanly
slides.speech: Through the framework, Martin is strong on the writing problem and on the limits of linear assumptions. The limits appear in claims-versus-evidence discipline and in not always centering the written sign as the primary unit.
slides.notes: This slide connects review to method.

image_prompts.concrete: Layer-level review slide separating Martin's SignWriting-positive insight, linearity critique, evidence limits, historical context, and full-path caution
image_prompts.abstract: A sympathetic comparison sorted into contribution, evidence boundary, and full-path caution

slides.title: Durable Contribution
slides.summary: Martin's durable value is that it sees SignWriting as a public writing possibility while showing how easily insight can outrun evidence.
bullets: Shows the cost of linear assumptions
slides.speech: Martin's durable value is that it sees SignWriting as a real public writing possibility. Its limits remind the platform to keep strong claims bounded by evidence.
slides.notes: Close as historical contribution plus caution.

image_prompts.concrete: Contribution slide showing Martin's early SignWriting-positive comparison preserved while dated software, learnability, and historical claims are bounded
image_prompts.abstract: A historical contribution kept intact while evidence limits remain visible
